en.extract:
The violet-bellied hummingbird (Chlorestes julie) is a species of hummingbird characterized by the male's shimmering violet belly. 


Taxonomy and systematics
The scientific name of this bird is currently Chlorestes julie, however, it was previously described as Juliamyia julie. Its genus being Chlorestes which is in the hummingbird family: Trochilidae. This family belongs to the order Apodiformes which includes hummingbirds and swifts. Within this species rank are three subspecies: panamensis, julie, and feliciana. Each are only separated by very minute features. Chlorestes julie panamensis occurs in Panama and has a head the same dull metallic green color as its back. Chlorestes julie julie and feliciana both have a brilliant green metallic back although julie has a shorter bill (14.5 mm) and a bluer belly whereas feliciana has a longer bill (15.1 mm) and a more violet belly. While julie occurs in Northern Columbia, feliciana occurs in western Ecuador and the extremely northwestern parts of Peru.


Description
The violet-bellied hummingbird is characterized by the male's shimmering violet belly. The rest of its body is just as brilliant; its back and crown being a metallic green.  The violet belly, however, is not shared between the sexes as this species exhibits sexual dimorphism. The characteristics they do share include long, rounded tails, straight and short bills, and pink lower mandibles.


Plumage
Adult:

The male violet-bellied hummingbird is more brightly colored than its female counterpart. Its breast and belly is a violet blue while its back, crown, and throat is a metallic green. While the rump is more of a bronze green. The rectrices are a blue black while the belly and undertail coverts are a dull black with a blue/green sheen. Around the tibia are white tufts which are framed by partly white spots on its belly. The feathers on its wings are dusky with a purple sheen. On average males are 7.5 cm long and weigh 3.4 g.
The female violet-bellied hummingbird is more dull than its male counterpart. Though its back, crown, and throat, is a similar metallic bronze green, its belly is a pale gray rather than a bright violet. Its rump and uppertail coverts are more bronze than green while undertail coverts are a dull brown gray. The rectrices can also be a blue black or a blueish green, and the outer rectrices are tipped with pale gray. Similar to the males, the females' wings are dusky with a purple sheen. Commonly in subspecies panamensis, the sides of the throat may be spotted with metallic green. On average females are 7 cm long and weigh 3.0 g.
Juvenile plumage has only been documented for immature males. They appear similar to an adult female although they may have some patches of violet on their belly or a sparkling green foreneck.
The molt pattern of this species is also yet to be described.


Audio


Similar species
Violet-bellied hummingbirds are very easily confused with woodnymphs. The green crowned woodnymph, in particular, has a similar color and shape and their ranges overlap. To tell them apart, you must check their size, color, and tail; violet-bellied hummingbirds are smaller, their backs are green instead of violet, and their tails are rounded, not notched. Green crowned woodnymphs also have a violet forecrown. Another similar species is the Sapphire-throated Hummingbird because of its colors. However, it has a blue throat, a green belly, and a notched tail.


Distribution and habitat

Violet-bellied hummingbirds are found in central Panama, through Columbia, extending to southwestern Ecuador and the extreme northwestern point of Peru. Different subspecies have different habitats within this range (see Taxonomy and Systematics section). None of which experience seasonal migration. This species is present in the understory of humid deciduous forests, forest edges, and regrowth forests.


Behavior and ecology


Breeding
As with many species of hummingbirds, violet-bellied hummingbirds seem to be polygynous. Males mate with multiple females and attract them by singing on perches about 1–10 m high. They may also fly in a 'u' shape in front of the females. However, after mating, they leave the female to rear the young. Individuals do not mate for life nor do they aggregate in flocks.
The females are responsible for creating nests and rearing the young. She creates a nest from plant fibers, animal hair and feathers in a shrub or a tree, approximately 1.2 to 4.2 m above ground. She will then incubate the clutch of two white eggs, each 8 by 13 mm. Incubation lasts 15 days followed by a 20–22 day nestling period. The chicks are altricial when they hatch and thus rely on their mother to feed and defend them. If successful, violet-bellied hummingbirds go on to live for approximately 4.2 years.


Food and feeding
Violet-bellied hummingbirds are primarily considered nectarivorous but may more accurately be called omnivorous. They consume nectar from plants in the Rubiaceae, Ericaceae, Gesneriaceae (Besleria), and Fabaceae (Inga) families. They also eat small arthropods, as do many other species of hummingbirds. Males may defend their feeding territory from other males and large insects. Although violet-bellied hummingbirds do not form flocks, they may congregate at fruit trees and feeders. The violet-bellied hummingbird's predators are not known.


Threats
According to their IUCN Red List conservation status of least concern, the violet-bellied hummingbird does not seem to be threatened. They are tolerant to disturbed areas such as forest edges.


In culture
Tourists and locals alike may join in bird watching tours around Panama where the violet-bellied hummingbird is one of the featured birds.


Status
The violet-bellied hummingbird is assessed as least concern by the IUCN Red List conservation status. Their population remains large and they have a wide distribution.


References


External links
Violet-bellied Hummingbird photo avesphoto

The '''violet-bellied hummingbird''' ('''''Chlorestes julie''''') is a species of [[hummingbird]] characterized by the male's shimmering violet belly.<ref name=":2" /> 

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The scientific name of this bird is currently ''Chlorestes julie,'' however, it was previously described as ''Juliamyia julie''.<ref name=":1" /> Its genus being ''Chlorestes'' which is in the hummingbird family: [[Hummingbird|Trochilidae]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Winkler|first1=David W.|last2=Billerman|first2=Shawn M.|last3=Lovette|first3=Irby J.|editor1-first=Shawn M|editor1-last=Billerman|editor2-first=Brooke K|editor2-last=Keeney|editor3-first=Paul G|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor4-first=Thomas S|editor4-last=Schulenberg|date=2020-03-04|title=Hummingbirds (Trochilidae)|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/trochi1/cur/introduction|journal=Birds of the World|language=en|doi=10.2173/bow.trochi1.01|url-access=subscription}}</ref> This family belongs to the order Apodiformes which includes hummingbirds and swifts.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Parker|first1=Charles Thomas|last2=Garrity|first2=George M|editor1-first=Charles Thomas|editor1-last=Parker|editor2-first=George M|editor2-last=Garrity|date=2003|title=Roseovarius halocynthiae, Aliiroseovarius halocynthiae, Pseudoroseovarius halocynthiae|website=The NamesforLife Abstracts|doi=10.1601/ex.22952}}</ref> Within this species rank are three subspecies: ''panamensis, julie,'' and ''feliciana''.<ref name=":2">{{Citation|last=Schulenberg|first=Thomas S.|title=Violet-bellied Hummingbird (Juliamyia julie)|date=2020-03-04|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/vibhum1/1.0/introduction|work=Birds of the World|editor-last=Billerman|editor-first=Shawn M.|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|language=en|doi=10.2173/bow.vibhum1.01|access-date=2020-10-11|editor2-last=Keeney|editor2-first=Brooke K.|editor3-last=Rodewald|editor3-first=Paul G.|editor4-last=Schulenberg|editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Each are only separated by very minute features. ''Chlorestes julie panamensis'' occurs in Panama and has a head the same dull metallic green color as its back.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Ridgway|first=Robert|title=Nomenclature of North American birds chiefly contained in the United States National Museum|date=1881|publisher=Govt. Print. Off.|location=Washington, DC|doi=10.5962/bhl.title.57047}}</ref> ''Chlorestes julie julie'' and ''feliciana'' both have a brilliant green metallic back although ''julie'' has a shorter bill (14.5 mm) and a bluer belly whereas ''feliciana'' has a longer bill (15.1 mm) and a more violet belly.<ref name=":1" /> While ''julie'' occurs in Northern Columbia, ''feliciana'' occurs in western Ecuador and the extremely northwestern parts of Peru.<ref name=":2" />

== Description ==
The violet-bellied hummingbird is characterized by the male's shimmering violet belly.<ref name=":2" /> The rest of its body is just as brilliant; its back and crown being a metallic green.<ref name=":2" />  The violet belly, however, is not shared between the sexes as this species exhibits [[sexual dimorphism]].<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|title=Avise's Birds of the World|url=https://avise-birds.bio.uci.edu/apodiformes/trochilidae/damophila_julie/index.html|access-date=2020-10-11|website=avise-birds.bio.uci.edu}}</ref> The characteristics they do share include long, rounded tails, straight and short bills, and pink lower mandibles.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":2" />

=== Plumage ===
Adult:

* The male violet-bellied hummingbird is more brightly colored than its female counterpart.<ref name=":2" /> Its breast and belly is a violet blue while its back, crown, and throat is a metallic green.<ref name=":2" /> While the rump is more of a bronze green.<ref name=":2" /> The rectrices are a blue black while the belly and undertail coverts are a dull black with a blue/green sheen.<ref name=":2" /> Around the tibia are white tufts which are framed by partly white spots on its belly.<ref name=":2" /> The feathers on its wings are dusky with a purple sheen.<ref name=":2" /> On average males are 7.5 cm long and weigh 3.4 g.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Ridgley|first1=Robert S.|last2=Greenfield|first2=Paul J.|date=2003|title=The Birds of Ecuador|journal=The Auk|volume=120|issue=2|pages=562–568|doi=10.2307/4090213|jstor=4090213|issn=0004-8038}}</ref><ref name=":2" />
* The female violet-bellied hummingbird is more dull than its male counterpart.<ref name=":2" /> Though its back, crown, and throat, is a similar metallic bronze green, its belly is a pale gray rather than a bright violet.<ref name=":2" /> Its rump and uppertail coverts are more bronze than green while undertail coverts are a dull brown gray.<ref name=":2" /> The rectrices can also be a blue black or a blueish green, and the outer rectrices are tipped with pale gray.<ref name=":2" /> Similar to the males, the females' wings are dusky with a purple sheen.<ref name=":2" /> Commonly in subspecies panamensis, the sides of the throat may be spotted with metallic green.<ref name=":2" /> On average females are 7 cm long and weigh 3.0 g.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":4" />

Juvenile plumage has only been documented for immature males. They appear similar to an adult female although they may have some patches of violet on their belly or a sparkling green foreneck.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" />

The molt pattern of this species is also yet to be described.

=== Audio ===
{{Birdsong|species=Violet-bellied hummingbird|url=https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Juliamyia-juli|align=center}}

=== Similar species ===
Violet-bellied hummingbirds are very easily confused with woodnymphs.<ref name=":2" /> The [[Green-crowned woodnymph|green crowned woodnymph]], in particular, has a similar color and shape and their ranges overlap.<ref name=":7">{{Cite book|last1=Athanas|first1=Nick|url=http://www.degruyter.com/view/books/9781400880706/9781400880706/9781400880706.xml|title=Birds of Western Ecuador: A Photographic Guide|last2=Greenfield|first2=Paul J.|date=2016-12-31|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-8070-6|location=Princeton|doi=10.1515/9781400880706}}</ref> To tell them apart, you must check their size, color, and tail; violet-bellied hummingbirds are smaller, their backs are green instead of violet, and their tails are rounded, not notched.<ref name=":2" /> Green crowned woodnymphs also have a violet forecrown.<ref name=":2" /> Another similar species is the [[Sapphire-throated hummingbird|Sapphire-throated Hummingbird]] because of its colors.<ref name=":2" /> However, it has a blue throat, a green belly, and a notched tail.<ref name=":2" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Violet-bellied Hummingbird - Ecuador S4E8390.jpg|thumb|A male violet-bellied hummingbird perched on a tree branch.]]
Violet-bellied hummingbirds are found in central Panama, through Columbia, extending to southwestern Ecuador and the extreme northwestern point of Peru.<ref name=":2" /> Different subspecies have different habitats within this range (see Taxonomy and Systematics section). None of which experience seasonal migration.<ref name=":2" /> This species is present in the understory of humid deciduous forests, forest edges, and regrowth forests.<ref name=":2" />

== Behavior and ecology ==

=== Breeding ===
As with many species of hummingbirds, violet-bellied hummingbirds seem to be [[Polygyny in animals|polygynous]].<ref name=":2" /> Males mate with multiple females and attract them by singing on perches about 1–10 m high.<ref name=":2" /> They may also fly in a 'u' shape in front of the females.<ref name=":5">{{Cite web|title=Violet-bellied Hummingbirds {{!}} Beauty of Birds|url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/violetbelliedhummingbirds.html|access-date=2020-10-12|website=www.beautyofbirds.com}}</ref> However, after mating, they leave the female to rear the young.<ref name=":5" /> Individuals do not mate for life nor do they aggregate in flocks.<ref name=":5" />

The females are responsible for creating nests and rearing the young.<ref name=":5" /> She creates a nest from plant fibers, animal hair and feathers in a shrub or a tree, approximately 1.2 to 4.2 m above ground.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":2" /> She will then incubate the clutch of two white eggs, each 8 by 13 mm.<ref name=":2" /> Incubation lasts 15 days followed by a 20–22 day nestling period.<ref name=":2" /> The chicks are [[Altriciality|altricial]] when they hatch and thus rely on their mother to feed and defend them.<ref name=":5" /> If successful, violet-bellied hummingbirds go on to live for approximately 4.2 years.<ref name=":6">{{Cite iucn|last=IUCN|date=2016|title=Juliamyia julie: The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|language=en|doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22687411A93150678.en}}</ref>

=== Food and feeding ===
Violet-bellied hummingbirds are primarily considered nectarivorous but may more accurately be called omnivorous.<ref name=":2" /> They consume nectar from plants in the [[Rubiaceae]], [[Ericaceae]], [[Gesneriaceae]] ([[Besleria]]), and [[Fabaceae]] ([[Inga]]) families.<ref name=":2" /> They also eat small arthropods, as do many other species of hummingbirds.<ref name=":2" /> Males may defend their feeding territory from other males and large insects.<ref name=":5" /> Although violet-bellied hummingbirds do not form flocks, they may congregate at fruit trees and feeders.<ref name=":7" /> The violet-bellied hummingbird's predators are not known.

=== Threats ===
According to their IUCN Red List conservation status of least concern, the violet-bellied hummingbird does not seem to be threatened.<ref name=":6" /> They are tolerant to disturbed areas such as forest edges.<ref name=":2" />

== In culture ==
Tourists and locals alike may join in bird watching tours around Panama where the violet-bellied hummingbird is one of the featured birds.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Panama Bird Watching Tours|url=https://www.justadventurespanama.com/copia-de-day-tours|access-date=2020-10-13|website=justadventures24|language=en}}</ref>

== Status ==
The violet-bellied hummingbird is assessed as least concern by the IUCN Red List conservation status.<ref name=":6" /> Their population remains large and they have a wide distribution.<ref name=":6" />

zh.extract:
紫腹蜂鸟（学名：Chlorestes julie）是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
紫腹蜂鸟体重约3.3克，翼长约43.1毫米，嘴峰长约16毫米，喙宽度约1毫米，喙厚度约1.6毫米，跗蹠长约4.1毫米，尾长约26毫米。紫腹蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在密集的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。


亚种
C. j. panamensis：分布于巴拿马中部。
C. j. julie：分布于哥伦比亚北部。
C. j. feliciana：分布于哥伦比亚西南部至厄瓜多尔西部和秘鲁极西北部。
